food_subgroup: Spiced and grilled vegetables — tandoori preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori-spiced grilled cauliflower; closest parent 'cauliflower food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Cauliflower) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for tandoori-marinated grilled cauliflower preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226734001 (Cauliflower) used as closest parent; no specific concept for tandoori preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Tandoori Gobi is approximately 200 g, comprising a portion of marinated and tandoor-grilled cauliflower florets served with mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Cauliflower, cooked; adapted for tandoori spiced preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked cauliflower used as the primary base. Macros adjusted upward to account for oil and yogurt marinade used in tandoori preparation, consistent with standard tandoori gobi recipe composition. Energy, fat, and protein increments reflect marinade contribution per 100 g edible portion. Sodium reflects added spice-salt blend typical of restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Vegetables, non-starchy; cauliflower analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori gobi. Cauliflower is a non-starchy vegetable with a GI of approximately 15 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The tandoori marinade (yogurt, spices, small amount of oil) does not materially alter the glycemic response. GI of 15 applied as the closest defensible analogous estimate. GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 9.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.guidance: Tandoori Gobi contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g due to the spiced marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of under 3 per serving, Tandoori Gobi is a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and can be included regularly as part of a balanced low-GI meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori Gobi is low in saturated fat (1.4 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre and anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric and cumin, making it a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal added oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Gobi is an Indian vegetarian dish consisting of cauliflower florets marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional tandoor clay oven. The result is a dish with lightly charred, smoky exterior florets that are tender inside, with a complex spiced flavour profile and a mild heat. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in North Indian restaurants, often accompanied by mint chutney and sliced onions. Nutritionally, Tandoori Gobi is a low-calorie, low-GI vegetable dish providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (3.8 g) from the yogurt marinade, modest fat (5.2 g), and useful dietary fibre (2.8 g). Its very low glycemic index of approximately 15 makes it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The spice blend contributes anti-inflammatory phytonutrients including curcumin from turmeric. In Singapore, Tandoori Gobi is served at Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay and is popular among vegetarians and health-conscious diners.
common_user_queries: Is gobi tandoori good for diabetics?
What spices are used in tandoori gobi?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for tandoori gobi as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked cauliflower data with marinade contribution estimated from standard recipe composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy cauliflower. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for cauliflower. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and standard recipe composition. Nutrient values represent the edible cooked portion including marinade contribution. Sodium of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level spiced preparation and may vary by establishment. The field survey source record listed this dish simply as 'Gobi' served at Kinara - Boat Quay; the canonical food name has been expanded to 'Tandoori Gobi' to reflect the described preparation method (tandoori clay oven grilling). Price data (SGD 14.00) preserved from source record for reference only and is not used in nutritional scoring.
